content:
Choose the correct metal/ ion from the brackets which -------------------------

A. has chemical reactivity similar to that of the first few members of the Lanthanoids $(\mathrm{Zn}, \mathrm{Ca}, \mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Cu})$.

B. has stable $4 \mathrm{f}^7$ electronic configuration, but acts as a strong reducing agent and converts to $\mathrm{M}^{3+}$ state. $\left(\mathrm{Eu}^{2+}, \mathrm{Ce}^{2+}, \mathrm{Pr}^{2+}, \mathrm{Dy}^{2+}\right)$

C. is a colorless ion $\left(\mathrm{Tm}^{3+}, \mathrm{Lu}^{3+}, \mathrm{Gd}^{3+}, \mathrm{Sm}^{3+}\right)$.

D. shows stable +2 oxidation state and is diamagnetic ( $\mathrm{Ce}, \mathrm{Sm}, \mathrm{Ho}, \mathrm{Yb}$ )

options.content:
A: Cu
B: $\mathrm{Dy}^{2+}$
C: $\mathrm{Sm}^{3+}$
D: Ho

A: Fe
B: $\mathrm{Pr}^{2+}$
C: $\mathrm{Tm}^{3+}$
D: Ce

explanation:
The only fully consistent choice is Option D:

A. Ca

– Lanthanoids are very electropositive, reactive metals; Ca behaves most like them.

B. $\mathrm{Eu}^{2+}$

– $\mathrm{Eu}^{2+}$ has a half‐filled $4f^7$ shell and readily oxidizes to $\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}$.

C. $\mathrm{Lu}^{3+}$

– With a full $4f^{14}$ configuration, $\mathrm{Lu}^{3+}$ has no $f\!\to\!f$ transitions in the visible and is colorless.

D. $\mathrm{Yb}^{2+}$

– $\mathrm{Yb}^{2+}$ is $4f^{14}$ (fully paired electrons) and so is diamagnetic.

content: The electronic configuration of the element with atomic number 78 is :
explanation:
Platinum (Z = 78) is one of the d-block exceptions that shifts an electron from 6s into 5d for extra stability. Its ground-state configuration is

Option A

$$[\mathrm{Xe}]\,4f^{14}\,5d^9\,6s^1$$

– the half-filled 6s and nearly filled 5d give a more stable arrangement than [Xe] 4f¹⁴ 5d⁸ 6s².

content:
Find the correct matches.

A. $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}$ P. is predominantly a basic compound.
B. $\mathrm{FeCr}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$ Q. is predominantly a acidic compound
C. $\mathrm{CrO}_3$ R. forms yellow chromate on reaction with $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3$ in air.
D. $\mathrm{CrO}$ S. is interconvertible with $\mathrm{MO}_4^{2-}$ depending on the pH of the medium.
explanation:
The only consistent matching is

A → S

B → R

C → Q

D → P

so the correct choice is D.

Briefly:

• Cr₂O₇²⁻ ⇌ 2 CrO₄²⁻ + 2 H⁺ (pH‐dependent interconversion) → S

• FeCr₂O₄ + Na₂CO₃ + O₂ → Na₂CrO₄ (yellow chromate) → R

• CrO₃ is a strongly acidic oxide → Q

• CrO is a basic oxide → P

content:
Choose the statements which are incorrect in the case of Lanthanoids.

A. $\mathrm{Ce}^{4+}$ is diamagnetic while $\mathrm{Sm}^{3+}$ is paramagnetic.

B. The atomic size of the transition metals having atomic number greater than 71 are very close to that of the elements above them.

C. Lanthanoids react with hot water forming water soluble $\mathrm{Ln}(\mathrm{OH})_3$ with the liberation of $\mathrm{O}_2$.

D. The general electronic configuration of Lanthanoids is $(n-2) \mathrm{f}^{1-14} 5 \mathrm{~d}^0 6 \mathrm{~s}^2$, where $n=6$.
explanation:
The incorrect statements are C and D (Option A).

Statement C is wrong because lanthanoids react with water to give hydrogen gas, not oxygen, and their hydroxides are sparingly soluble.

$$2\,\mathrm{Ln} + 6\,\mathrm{H_2O}\;\longrightarrow\;2\,\mathrm{Ln(OH)_3} + 3\,\mathrm{H_2}$$

Statement D is too restrictive. While many lanthanoids follow

$$[\mathrm{Xe}]\,4f^n5d^0 6s^2,$$

several (La, Ce, Gd, etc.) actually have a 5d electron:

– La: $$[\mathrm{Xe}]\,5d^1 6s^2\quad(f^0)$$

– Ce: $$[\mathrm{Xe}]\,4f^1 5d^1 6s^2$$

– Gd: $$[\mathrm{Xe}]\,4f^7 5d^1 6s^2$$

Statements A and B are correct.

content: Larger number of oxidation states are exhibited by the actinoids than those of lanthanoids. The reason is:

options.content: Lesser energy difference between 5 f and 6 d than between 4 f and 5 d orbitals
More energy difference between 5 f and 6 d than between 4 f and 5 d orbitals
4 f orbitals are more diffused than 5 f orbitals
Highly reactive nature of the actinoids

explanation:
The correct answer is Option A.

Reasoning:

In actinoids, the energy gap between the 5f and 6d orbitals is relatively small.

Because these orbitals lie close in energy, electrons from both 5f and 6d (and even 7s) can participate in bonding.

This flexibility allows actinoids to exhibit a wide range of oxidation states (from +3 up to +7 in early actinoids).

By contrast, in the lanthanoids the 4f–5d energy gap is much larger. The deeply buried 4f electrons are less available for bonding, so lanthanoids are mostly restricted to the +3 state (with a few +2 or +4 exceptions).

content:
Which is the correct order of increasing number of unpaired electrons in the following ions?

$$\mathrm{A}=\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}(\mathrm{Z}=24) \mathrm{B}=\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(\mathrm{Z}=29) \quad \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}(\mathrm{Z}=28) \mathrm{D}=\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}(\mathrm{Z}=26)$$
explanation:
Let’s work out the d-electron counts and unpaired electrons in each ion:

Cr²⁺ (Z = 24)

– Neutral Cr: $$[\mathrm{Ar}]\,3d^5\,4s^1$$

– Cr²⁺ loses the 4s electron and one 3d electron → $$[\mathrm{Ar}]\,3d^4$$

– Four d-electrons occupy four orbitals singly ⇒ 4 unpaired electrons

Fe³⁺ (Z = 26)

– Neutral Fe: $$[\mathrm{Ar}]\,3d^6\,4s^2$$

– Fe³⁺ loses two 4s and one 3d → $$[\mathrm{Ar}]\,3d^5$$

– Five d-electrons each in a separate orbital ⇒ 5 unpaired electrons

Ni²⁺ (Z = 28)

– Neutral Ni: $$[\mathrm{Ar}]\,3d^8\,4s^2$$

– Ni²⁺ loses the two 4s electrons → $$[\mathrm{Ar}]\,3d^8$$

– Hund’s rule: 5 orbitals get 5 electrons singly, the remaining 3 pair up ⇒ 2 unpaired electrons

Cu²⁺ (Z = 29)

– Neutral Cu: $$[\mathrm{Ar}]\,3d^{10}\,4s^1$$

– Cu²⁺ loses 4s and one 3d → $$[\mathrm{Ar}]\,3d^9$$

– Nine electrons fill 4 orbitals completely and leave 1 singly occupied ⇒ 1 unpaired electron

Summarizing unpaired electrons:

• Cu²⁺ (B): 1

• Ni²⁺ (C): 2

• Cr²⁺ (A): 4

• Fe³⁺ (D): 5

So in order of increasing unpaired electrons:

B < C < A < D

That corresponds to Option A.

content:
Arrange the following ions in the decreasing order of covalent double bonds formed by the transition metal with oxygen.

A. $=$ chromate $\mathrm{B}=$ permanganate $\mathrm{C}=$ dichromate

Match the metals in Column II with their characteristic properties given in Column I.

Properties Metals
A. Lanthanoid with low value of $3^{\text {rd }}$ ionisation enthalpy. P V
B. f-Block element with electronic configuration $[R n] 6 d^2 7 s^2$ Q Gd
C. 3 d -series metal whose $\mathrm{M}^{3+}{ }_{(\mathrm{aq})}$ is colourless. R Th
D. 3d-series metal with highest $\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {(atomisation) }}$ S Sc
explanation:
Here’s the match‐up with brief reasoning:

A. “Lanthanoid with low 3rd ionisation enthalpy”

– The only lanthanoid in the list is Gd (Q).

⇒ A → Q

B. “f-Block element with configuration $$[Rn]\,6d^2\,7s^2$$”

– That is thorium (Th), which is R.

⇒ B → R

C. “3d-series metal whose M³⁺₍ₐq₎ is colourless”

– Sc³⁺ (d⁰) is colourless. Sc is S.

⇒ C → S

D. “3d-series metal with the highest ΔH₍atomisation₎”

– Vanadium (V) has the highest atomisation enthalpy. V is P.

⇒ D → P

So the correct choice is Option A:

A = Q, B = R, C = S, D = P.

content: Choose the correct statement.

options.content: Ionic compounds of $\mathrm{Sc}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{Cu}^{+}$are coloured because of $\mathrm{d}-\mathrm{d}$ electronic transitions

The order in which the paramagnetic nature of the 4 cations $\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}, \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}, \mathrm{V}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ vary is $\mathrm{V}^{2+}<\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}=\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}<\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$
As the oxidation number of the transition element increases, the ionic nature decreases and the oxides show acidic nature predominantly
The metal Cobalt has the electronic configuration $[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^5$ in the +3 oxidation state

explanation:
Option C is correct.

• As the oxidation state of a transition metal increases, its cation becomes more polarizing, so the compound’s ionic character goes down (it becomes more covalent).

• Higher‐oxidation‐state oxides tend to be acidic, whereas lower‐oxidation‐state oxides are more basic.

content: What is the spin only magnetic moment of the metal ion in $\mathbf{P}$ and the oxidation number of Sulphur in the oxidised product Z ?
Identify the correct statement from the following.

options.content: The green manganate ion shows diamagnetic nature but the permanganate ion exhibits paramagnetic nature
Interstitial compounds of transition metals have lower melting points than that of pure transition metals and their compounds are chemically reactive
Cerium is a lanthanoid metal which exists in a stable oxidation state of +4 , besides exhibiting an oxidation state of +3
$$\mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{VI})$$ is more stable than $$\mathrm{W}(\mathrm{VI})$$ and hence acts as a good oxidising agent

explanation:
Each of the given statements needs to be examined carefully to identify the correct one. Let's analyze them one by one:

Option A: The green manganate ion shows diamagnetic nature but the permanganate ion exhibits paramagnetic nature.

This statement is not correct because both the manganate ion ($$\mathrm{MnO_4^{2-}}$$) and the permanganate ion ($$\mathrm{MnO_4^-}$$) are paramagnetic in nature, as both contain unpaired electrons in their d-orbitals.

Option B: Interstitial compounds of transition metals have lower melting points than that of pure transition metals and their compounds are chemically reactive.

This statement is also not correct. Interstitial compounds typically have higher melting points compared to their pure metals due to the presence of small atoms (like hydrogen, carbon, or nitrogen) in the interstices of the metal lattice structure, which enhances the metallic bonding. Additionally, they are generally less reactive than their constituent metals.

Option C: Cerium is a lanthanoid metal which exists in a stable oxidation state of +4, besides exhibiting an oxidation state of +3.

This statement is correct. Cerium (Ce) indeed has two common oxidation states: +3 and +4. The +3 oxidation state is more common for lanthanoids, but cerium is unique in that it can exist stably in the +4 oxidation state as well.

Option D: $$\mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{VI})$$ is more stable than $$\mathrm{W}(\mathrm{VI})$$ and hence acts as a good oxidising agent.

This statement is not correct. In fact, $$\mathrm{W}(\mathrm{VI})$$ is generally more stable than $$\mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{VI})$$. Chromium (VI) compounds are strong oxidizing agents, indicating they are less stable and readily reduced.

Therefore, the correct statement is:

Option C: Cerium is a lanthanoid metal which exists in a stable oxidation state of +4, besides exhibiting an oxidation state of +3.

content:
An inorganic compound $$\mathrm{W}$$ undergoes the following reactions:

$$ \begin{gathered} W+\frac{\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3}{\mathrm{O}_2 / \text { heat }} \rightarrow X+\frac{\mathrm{H}^{+}}{} \rightarrow Y_{(s)} \\ Y(a q)+\mathrm{KCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow Z_{(S)} \end{gathered} $$

Z appears in the form of orange crystals and is used as an oxidising agent in acid medium. Identify the compound W.
explanation:
To identify the compound $$\mathrm{W}$$, let's examine the given reactions step by step:

1. The first reaction is:

$$ \mathrm{W} + \frac{\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{CO}_3}{\mathrm{O}_2 / \text { heat }} \rightarrow \mathrm{X} $$

This reaction involves an inorganic compound $$\mathrm{W}$$ being treated with sodium carbonate and oxygen under heat to form a compound $$\mathrm{X}$$. This is a classic method employed to oxidize a compound. One known example is the oxidation of chromite ore where $$\mathrm{FeCr}_2\mathrm{O}_4$$ (chromite) gets oxidized to sodium chromate ($$\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{CrO}_4$$).

2. The second step:

$$ \mathrm{X} + \mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow \mathrm{Y}_{(s)} $$

Adding acid H+ to sodium chromate ($$\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{CrO}_4$$) converts it to a solid dichromate compound. In aqueous solution, sodium chromate is converted to sodium dichromate ($$\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{Cr_2O_7}$$), which precipitates as a solid in a more concentrated solution.

3. The third reaction is:

$$ \mathrm{Y}(\mathrm{aq}) + \mathrm{KCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow \mathrm{Z}_{(\mathrm{s})} $$

Here, when the resulting dichromate solution reacts with potassium chloride (KCl), potassium dichromate ($$\mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{Cr_2O_7}$$) forms as a precipitate. Potassium dichromate is known for its orange crystals and is widely used as an oxidizing agent in acidic medium.

Based on these reactions, we can see the transformation of chromite $$\mathrm{FeCr}_2\mathrm{O}_4$$ into $$\mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{Cr_2O_7}$$ through intermediates.

Thus, the compound $$\mathrm{W}$$ is:

Option B: $$\mathrm{FeCr_2O_4}$$

content:
The Lanthanoid ion which would form coloured compounds is -------------.

Atomic numbers: $$\mathrm{Yb}=70, \quad \mathrm{Lu}=71, \mathrm{Pr}=59, \quad \mathrm{La}=57$$
explanation:
The Lanthanoid ion which forms coloured compounds typically does so because of the presence of unpaired electrons in its f-orbitals. These unpaired electrons can absorb visible light, resulting in coloured compounds.

Let's analyze the given lanthanoid ions:

1. $$Yb^{2+}$$ (Ytterbium)

  • Ytterbium has an atomic number of 70.

  • In the $$Yb^{2+}$$ ion, the electronic configuration would be $$[Xe]4f^{14}$$.

  • Since there are no unpaired electrons in the 4f orbital (all 14 electrons are paired), $$Yb^{2+}$$ would form colourless compounds.

2. $$La^{3+}$$ (Lanthanum)

  • Lanthanum has an atomic number of 57.

  • In the $$La^{3+}$$ ion, the electronic configuration would be $$[Xe]4f^0$$.

  • Since there are no electrons in the 4f orbital, $$La^{3+}$$ would form colourless compounds.

3. $$Lu^{3+}$$ (Lutetium)

  • Lutetium has an atomic number of 71.

  • In the $$Lu^{3+}$$ ion, the electronic configuration would be $$[Xe]4f^{14}$$.

  • As with $$Yb^{2+}$$, all 14 electrons are paired, so $$Lu^{3+}$$ would also form colourless compounds.

4. $$Pr^{3+}$$ (Praseodymium)

  • Praseodymium has an atomic number of 59.

  • In the $$Pr^{3+}$$ ion, the electronic configuration would be $$[Xe]4f^2$$.

  • There are 2 unpaired electrons in the 4f orbital, which can interact with visible light and result in the formation of coloured compounds.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option D

$$\operatorname{Pr}^{3+}$$

content:
The metallic ions that have almost same spin only magnetic moment are :

(i) $$\mathrm{Co}^{2+}$$

(ii) $$\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$$

(iii) $$\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}$$

(iv) $$\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$$
explanation:
To determine which ions have almost the same spin-only magnetic moment, we need to look at the number of unpaired electrons in each ion and then calculate their magnetic moments using the spin-only formula:

$$\mu_{so} = \sqrt{n(n+2)}$$

where:

$$n$$ is the number of unpaired electrons.

$$\mu_{so}$$ is the magnetic moment in Bohr magnetons (BM).

Let’s analyze each ion:

$$\mathrm{Co}^{2+}$$

Electronic configuration: For cobalt ($$Z = 27$$), $$\mathrm{Co}^{2+}$$ typically has a $$3d^7$$ configuration.

In a high-spin octahedral complex, it has 3 unpaired electrons.

Spin-only magnetic moment:

$$\mu_{so} = \sqrt{3(3+2)} = \sqrt{15} \approx 3.87 \text{ BM}.$$

$$\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$$

Electronic configuration: Manganese ($$Z = 25$$) gives a $$3d^5$$ configuration for $$\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$$.

It has 5 unpaired electrons (high-spin is common).

Spin-only magnetic moment:

$$\mu_{so} = \sqrt{5(5+2)} = \sqrt{35} \approx 5.92 \text{ BM}.$$

$$\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}$$

Electronic configuration: For chromium ($$Z = 24$$), $$\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}$$ usually has a $$3d^4$$ configuration.

It has 4 unpaired electrons in the high-spin state.

Spin-only magnetic moment:

$$\mu_{so} = \sqrt{4(4+2)} = \sqrt{24} \approx 4.90 \text{ BM}.$$

$$\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$$

Electronic configuration: $$\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$$ has a $$3d^3$$ configuration.

It has 3 unpaired electrons.

Spin-only magnetic moment:

$$\mu_{so} = \sqrt{3(3+2)} = \sqrt{15} \approx 3.87 \text{ BM}.$$

Now, comparing the magnetic moments:

$$\mathrm{Co}^{2+}$$ and $$\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$$ both have approximately $$3.87 \text{ BM}.$$

$$\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$$ and $$\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}$$ have significantly higher values (around $$5.92 \text{ BM}$$ and $$4.90 \text{ BM}$$ respectively).

Therefore, the metallic ions with almost the same spin-only magnetic moment are:

$$\mathrm{Co}^{2+} \text{ and } \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}.$$

This corresponds to Option C: (i) and (iv).

content: The correct order of increasing melting point is
explanation:
We can compare the approximate melting points (in °C) of the given elements:

• Manganese (Mn) ~ 1246°C

• Titanium (Ti) ~ 1668°C

• Chromium (Cr) ~ 1907°C

• Vanadium (V) ~ 1910°C

Thus, when we order them from lowest to highest, we have:

$$ \text{Mn (1246°C)} < \text{Ti (1668°C)} < \text{Cr (1907°C)} < \text{V (1910°C)} $$

However, note that the melting points of chromium and vanadium are extremely close (differing by only about 3°C); in many texts and competitive exam questions the order is given using one of these two as the highest. Among the provided options, only Option B begins with manganese as the lowest and titanium following it. Option B gives:

$$ \text{Mn} < \text{Ti} < \text{V} < \text{Cr} $$

This is the answer most commonly accepted in such questions, even though some sources might slightly swap the last two due to the close values.

Thus, the answer is Option B.

content:
Consider the following statements in respect of lanthanides, which of the statements are incorrect?

(i) $$\mathrm{La}(\mathrm{OH})_3$$ is least basic among the hydroxides of lanthanides

(ii) The lanthanide ions $$\mathrm{Yb}^{2+}, \mathrm{Lu}^{3+}$$ and $$\mathrm{Ce}^{4+}$$ are diamagnetic in nature.

(iii) $$\mathrm{Ce}^{4+}$$ can act as an oxidising agent

(iv) $$\operatorname{Ln}$$ (III) compounds are generally colourless

(v) Ionic radii of $$\mathrm{Ce}^{3+}$$ is greater than $$\mathrm{Yb}^{3+}$$
explanation:
Let’s examine each statement one by one:

Regarding (i):

 – The basicity of a metal hydroxide depends on how ionic its bonding is. In lanthanide(III) hydroxides, a larger ionic radius means the metal–oxygen bond is more ionic and the compound is more readily dissociable in water (i.e. more basic).

 – Since $$\mathrm{La}^{3+}$$ is the largest among the 4f-block elements, $$\mathrm{La(OH)}_3$$ is comparatively more ionic and hence more basic.

 – The statement claims that $$\mathrm{La(OH)}_3$$ is the least basic, which is incorrect.

For (ii):

 – $$\mathrm{Yb}^{2+}$$ has the configuration $$4f^{14}$$, $$\mathrm{Lu}^{3+}$$ has $$4f^{14}$$, and $$\mathrm{Ce}^{4+}$$, after losing its 4f electron, has an empty 4f shell.

 – In each case all electrons are paired, making these ions diamagnetic.

 – Therefore, (ii) is correct.

Concerning (iii):

 – $$\mathrm{Ce}^{4+}$$ is well known as a strong oxidising agent (for example, it is used in ceric ammonium nitrate).

 – Thus, (iii) is correct.

Discussing (iv):

 – Although the $$4f$$–$$4f$$ transitions in lanthanide(III) ions are Laporte forbidden and thus give very weak absorption bands, many $$\mathrm{Ln}^{3+}$$ ions (those with partially filled 4f shells) do exhibit a pale color in their compounds.

 – In fact, only those with $$f^0$$, $$f^7$$, or $$f^{14}$$ configurations (like $$\mathrm{La}^{3+}, \mathrm{Gd}^{3+}, \mathrm{Lu}^{3+}$$) are truly colorless. The majority of lanthanide(III) compounds show characteristic hues—even if faint.

 – Therefore, the broad statement that $$\mathrm{Ln(III)}$$ compounds are generally colorless is not accurate.

Finally, (v):

 – The lanthanide contraction means that ionic radii decrease from left to right in the series.

 – Hence, the ionic radius of $$\mathrm{Ce}^{3+}$$ (an early lanthanide) is indeed larger than that of $$\mathrm{Yb}^{3+}$$ (a later one).

 – So (v) is correct.

In summary, the incorrect statements are (i) and (iv).

The correct answer is Option B.

content:
Arrange the following ions in the increasing order of their $$\Delta H_{(\text {hydration})}$$ values.

$$\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}, \mathrm{Co}^{2+}, \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}, \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}$$
explanation:
The hydration enthalpy ($$\Delta H_{(\text {hydration})}$$) of ions generally depends on the charge density of the ions. Higher charge density results in stronger interactions with water molecules and thus a more negative (exothermic) hydration enthalpy.

The charge density is influenced by both the charge and the ionic radius: smaller ions with the same charge will have higher charge density and thus a more negative $$\Delta H_{(\text {hydration})}$$.

For the given ions:

$$\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}, \mathrm{Co}^{2+}, \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}, \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}$$

We need to consider the ionic radii to determine the order of hydration enthalpy. Typically, the ionic radii decrease across the period from left to right.

Given the position of these elements in the periodic table, the approximate order of ionic radii (from largest to smallest) is:

$$\mathrm{Mn}^{2+} > \mathrm{Cr}^{2+} > \mathrm{Co}^{2+} > \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}$$

Since smaller ionic radii correspond to higher charge density and thus more negative hydration enthalpy, the order of $$\Delta H_{(\text {hydration})}$$ values (from less negative to more negative) will be:

$$\mathrm{Mn}^{2+} < \mathrm{Cr}^{2+} < \mathrm{Co}^{2+} < \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}$$

Hence, the correct option is:

Option D

$$\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}<\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}<\mathrm{Co}^{2+}<\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}$$

content: The permanganate ion in acid medium acts as an oxidant and gets converted to its lower oxidation state. What would be the spin only magnetic moment of such reduced manganese ion?
explanation:
In order to find the spin-only magnetic moment of the reduced manganese ion, we need to follow a step-by-step process to determine the oxidation states and then compute the magnetic moment.

1. The permanganate ion ($$\text{MnO}_4^-$$) has manganese in the +7 oxidation state. In an acidic medium, it typically gets reduced to $$\text{Mn}^{2+}$$.

2. To find the magnetic moment, we first need to determine the number of unpaired electrons in the $$\text{Mn}^{2+}$$ ion. Manganese in its elemental form has the electron configuration $$[Ar]3d^54s^2$$.

3. For $$\text{Mn}^{2+}$$, the ion will lose two electrons from the $$4s$$ orbital, giving it the electron configuration $$[Ar]3d^5$$. This configuration represents 5 unpaired electrons.

4. The spin-only magnetic moment is given by the formula:

$$\mu = \sqrt{n(n+2)} \text{ BM}$$

where $$n$$ is the number of unpaired electrons.

5. Substituting $$n = 5$$ for $$\text{Mn}^{2+}$$:

$$\mu = \sqrt{5(5+2)} = \sqrt{35} \text{ BM}$$

6. Calculate the value:

$$\mu = \sqrt{35} \approx 5.92 \text{ BM}$$

Therefore, the spin-only magnetic moment of the reduced manganese ion in the $$\text{Mn}^{2+}$$ state is approximately 5.92 BM, which corresponds to option D.

content:
Match the compounds given in Column I with their characteristic features listed in Column II

No. Column I No. Column II
A $$
\mathrm{La}(\mathrm{OH})_3
$$		 P Acidic in nature
B $$
\mathrm{Mn}_2 \mathrm{O}_7
$$		 Q Least basic
C $$
\mathrm{Lu}(\mathrm{OH}) 3
$$		 R Interstitial compound
D $$
\mathrm{Fe}_3 \mathrm{H}
$$		 S Most basic
explanation:
Let's analyze the compounds and their characteristic features from Column I and Column II respectively and match them accordingly:

  1. $$\mathrm{La}(\mathrm{OH})_3$$ - This lanthanum hydroxide is typically considered as one of the most basic hydroxides among the lanthanides. Thus, it should be matched with "Most basic."

  1. $$\mathrm{Mn}_2\mathrm{O}_7$$ - This manganese heptoxide is highly oxidizing and is acidic in nature. Hence, it should be matched with "Acidic in nature."

  1. $$\mathrm{Lu}(\mathrm{OH})_3$$ - Lutetium hydroxide is considered to be the least basic among the hydroxides of lanthanide elements, therefore it should be matched with "Least basic."

  1. $$\mathrm{Fe}_3\mathrm{H}$$ - This iron compound is an example of an interstitial compound, thus it matches with "Interstitial compound."

Hence the correct matching is:

$$ \mathrm{A} = \mathrm{S} \quad \mathrm{B} = \mathrm{P} \quad \mathrm{C} = \mathrm{Q} \quad \mathrm{D} = \mathrm{R} $$

Thus, the correct option is Option A.

content: Choose the incorrect statement from the following

options.content: The ability of Fluorine to stabilise the higher oxidation states of transition metals exceeds that of Oxygen
$$\mathrm{Cu}$$ (I) compounds in aqueous medium undergo disproportionation reaction
$$\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}$$ is a stronger reducing agent than $$\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$$

content: $$\mathrm{Ti}^{2+}$$ is purple while $$\mathrm{Ti}^{4+}$$ is colourless because

options.content: $$\mathrm{Ti}^{2+}$$ is very small cation when compared to $$\mathrm{Ti}^{2+}$$ and hence, doesn't absorb any radiation
There is no crystal field effect in $$\mathrm{Ti}^{4+}$$

explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{Ti}(22) & =[\mathrm{Ar}] 4 s^2 3 d^2 \\ \mathrm{Ti}^{2+} & =[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^2 \\ \mathrm{Ti}^{4+} & =[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\mathrm{Ti}^{2+}$$ has two unpaired electrons in $$3 d$$ orbital and hence $$d$$-$$d$$ transition is possible due to absorption of light in visible region whereas $$\mathrm{Ti}^{4+}$$ is diamagnetic hence colourless.

\mathrm{C_{60}}
$$		 D Electron precise species, neither acts as electron donor nor as electron acceptor.
Identify the incorrect statement.

options.content: Ability of Fluorine to stabilise higher oxidation states in transition metals is due to the low lattice enthalpy of the fluorides.
The second and third Ionisation enthalpies of $$\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$$ and $$\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$$ respectively have lower values than expected.
Transition metals readily form alloys because their metallic radii are within about $$15 \%$$ of each other.
$$\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}$$ acts as reducing agent while $$\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}$$ acts as an oxidising agent though both the ions have $$\mathrm{d}^4$$ configuration.

explanation:
Each statement provided interacts with different aspects of transition metal chemistry. We will analyze each statement to identify the incorrect one:

Option A: "Ability of Fluorine to stabilise higher oxidation states in transition metals is due to the low lattice enthalpy of the fluorides."

This statement is incorrect. Fluorine's ability to stabilize higher oxidation states in transition metals is primarily because of its very high electronegativity and small ionic size, which allows it to form highly stable ionic bonds with metals in high oxidation states. The factor of lattice enthalpy does influence the stability of the fluoro-complexes, but it is mainly the strong ionic character and high electron-withdrawing capacity of fluorine that dominates this stabilizing effect. Additionally, fluorides often have higher lattice enthalpies due to strong ionic interactions, contrary to the statement's claim of "low lattice enthalpy."

Option B: "The second and third Ionisation enthalpies of $$\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$$ and $$\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$$ respectively have lower values than expected."

This statement is correct. The lower than expected ionization enthalpies for $$\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$$ (to form $$\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}$$) and $$\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$$ (to form $$\mathrm{Fe}^{4+}$$) can be linked to the electronic configurations and the relative stability imparted by half-filled or filled subshells that are achieved post-ionization. $$\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$$ has a $$3\mathrm{d}^5$$ configuration while $$\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$$ has a $$3\mathrm{d}^5$$ configuration, both of which are relatively stable.

Option C: "Transition metals readily form alloys because their metallic radii are within about $$15 \%$$ of each other."

This statement is correct. The similar atomic sizes of transition metals (often within about 15% of each other) contribute to their ability to substitute one another in a crystalline structure without significantly distorting the lattice, hence promoting the formation of alloys.

Option D: "$$\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}$$ acts as reducing agent while $$\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}$$ acts as an oxidising agent though both the ions have $$\mathrm{d}^4$$ configuration."

This statement is also correct. The oxidizing and reducing behavior of these ions is largely determined by their respective electronic configurations and associated redox potentials. Despite having the same electron count ($$d^4$$), $$\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}$$ is more inclined to accept electrons and be oxidized to $$\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$$ (a more stable half-filled $$d^3$$ state), making it a good reducing agent. On the other hand, $$\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}$$ prefers to lose an electron to form more stable $$\mathrm{Mn}^{4+}$$ (with $$d^3$$ configuration), functioning as an oxidizing agent.

Thus, Option A is the incorrect statement according to transition metal chemistry.

content: An aqueous solution of X on addition of hydrogen peroxide in ice cold conditions gives blue colour to the ethereal layer. Then, X can be
explanation:
Both chromate and dichromate radicals on reaction with acidified H$$_2$$O$$_2$$ give blue solution which turns green on standing. In the presence of organic solvents such as diethyl ether, amyl alcohol or pyridine, permanent blue colour obtained.

$$\mathrm{{K_2}C{r_2}{O_4} + {H_2}S{O_4} + {H_2}{O_2}\buildrel {Ether} \over \longrightarrow Cr{O_5} + {K_2}S{O_4} + 3{H_2}O}$$

$$\mathrm{{K_2}C{r_2}{O_7} + 4{H_2}S{O_4} + 4{H_2}{O_2}\buildrel {Ether} \over \longrightarrow \mathop {2Cr{O_5}}\limits_{(Blue\,colour)} + {K_2}S{O_4} + 5{H_2}O}$$

content: In 3d-transmission series, which one has the least melting point?
explanation:
The melting point of d-block elements is due to the presence of unpaired electrons in the last d-orbital or s-orbital. So, the element which has all the electrons in pairs will have the least melting point.

$$\bullet$$ Vanadium (V) with atomic number 23.

[Ar] 3d$$^3$$ 4s$$^2$$ has 3 unpaired electrons.

$$\bullet$$ Zinc (Zn) with atomic number 30.

[Ar] 3d$$^{10}$$ 4s$$^2$$ has zero unpaired electron.

$$\bullet$$ Manganese (Mn) with atomic number 25.

[Ar] 3d$$^5$$ 4s$$^2$$ has five unpaired electrons.

$$\bullet$$ Copper (Cu) with atomic number 29.

[Ar] 3d$$^{10}$$ 4s$$^1$$ has one unpaired electron.

Therefore, zinc has least melting point.

content: The electronic configuration of Cr$$^{3+}$$ is
explanation:
Electronic configuration of Cr = $$[Ar]3d^54s^1$$

Electronic configuration of Cr$$^{3+}$$ = $$[Ar]3d^34s^0$$

content: Formation of coloured solution is possible, when metal ion in the compound contains
explanation: Formation of coloured solution is possible, when metal ion in the compound contains unpaired electrons due to transition of electrons in excited state.

content: Which of the following forms a colourless solution in aqueous medium?
explanation:
The electronic configuration of $$\mathrm{Sc}$$ is

$$1 s^2 2 s^2 2 p^6 3 s^2 3 p^6 3 d^1 4 s^2$$

Electronic configuration of $$\mathrm{Sc}^{3+}$$ is $$1 s^2 2 s^2 2 p^6 3 s^2 3 p^6$$.

So, $$\mathrm{Sc}^{3+}$$ is colourless due to the absence of unpaired electrons in $$d$$-orbital.
